narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try based on academic and background factors, estimated a 41.9% chance that you would not pass—above the threshold we use for admission decisions. This prediction stems from a careful look at key elements of your profile, like your undergraduate GPA, family income bracket, and early academic performance in school. Among the factors the model weighed most heavily, your undergraduate GPA of 3.20 stood out as a modest strength, just slightly below the 3.24 average for students who passed the bar; it nudged the prediction toward expecting you to pass rather than fail. Similarly, your upper-middle family income aligned well with the majority of successful bar takers (about 45% of whom come from that bracket), providing another gentle push in the positive direction. However, your first-year decile ranking of 1 placed you at the lower end compared to the average of 6.04 for those who passed, which had the strongest pull toward predicting failure and tipped the overall balance. Your LSAT score of 26.50, well below the 37.27 average for passers, and third-year decile of 2 (versus 5.91 average) also factored in but less decisively, while your choice to study full-time matched the norm for 93% of successful students. In essence, while some aspects like your GPA and family background offered support, your early academic struggles carried the most weight in signaling higher risk to the model. Compared to typical admitted students—who generally show stronger early rankings and higher test scores—your profile appeared riskier for bar success, even though elements like your full-time commitment were favorable. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background, but a data-driven call to prioritize bar passage odds for incoming classes. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to the passing average, strengthening your early academic record through additional undergrad coursework if possible, and gathering letters highlighting your resilience and growth since first year. Many students in similar positions improve these areas and succeed on later applications—reach out to admissions advisors for personalized strategies, and keep pursuing your goals with that determination.
